title: Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption: Across countries in 2020, the Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) and GDP per capita show a positive relationship, with higher income levels generally coinciding with higher scores on the index. The scatter plot places GDP per capita on a logarithmic horizontal axis ranging from $1,000 to $100,000, while the vertical axis measures the AHDI from 0 to 0.8. A dense cluster of points occupies the lower-left quadrant, where nations such as Burundi, DR Congo, Liberia, and Malawi record GDP per capita below $2,000 and AHDI scores below 0.3. Moving toward the center of the distribution, countries like India, Pakistan, and Indonesia sit in the $5,000 to $10,000 range with index values between 0.3 and 0.5. In the upper-right section, high-income economies including Switzerland, the United States, and Japan display AHDI scores above 0.8 and GDP per capita exceeding $40,000. Several outliers deviate from the central trend line; Qatar and Kuwait appear with GDP per capita near or above $100,000 but AHDI scores around 0.4, while Equatorial Guinea shows a similar pattern with high income and a lower index score. Conversely, Moldova and Peru achieve index scores around 0.5 with GDP per capita figures closer to $10,000. The size of the circles represents population, with large markers visible for China, India, and the United States, contrasting with smaller dots for nations like Hong Kong and Switzerland. The data points form a curve that rises steeply at lower income levels and flattens as GDP per capita increases, showing that gains in the index become smaller at the highest income tiers.
